name: Saurabh
summary: Saurabh shares his inspiring journey from being an ordinary boy from Rohtak, Haryana, to clearing the NDA written examination and getting recommended by 11 SSB Allahabad in his very first attempt despite family opposition.
data:
# From an Ordinary Boy in Rohtak to Clearing the NDA SSB in My First Attempt

I was just an ordinary, average guy from Rohtak, Haryana. Like countless other students, I had dreams, ambitions, and uncertainties. But unlike most, there was one dream that had lived with me since childhood—to become an officer in the Indian Armed Forces.

The reason behind this dream wasn't just patriotism; it was the environment I grew up in. During my childhood, I had the opportunity to stay around places like the National Defence Academy (NDA), Rashtriya Indian Military College (RIMC), and other military training establishments. Later, spending nearly two years in Srinagar exposed me even more closely to the life of the armed forces. Every interaction, every parade, every officer I met strengthened my desire to wear the uniform one day.

There was, however, one obstacle that seemed impossible to overcome—my father.

Being the only child in my family, my parents never wanted me to join the Army. My father was especially against it. No matter how much I wanted to appear for the NDA examination, he never allowed me to even fill out the application form.

As my final NDA attempt approached, I decided that if I didn't try, I would regret it for the rest of my life. Without telling my parents, I secretly filled out the NDA form. On the day of the examination, I told them I was appearing for a private college entrance exam called MIT. Thankfully, my JEE preparation proved to be extremely helpful, and in my very first—and also my last eligible—attempt, I cleared the NDA written examination.

A month before my SSB interview, I finally gathered the courage to tell my parents the truth. I told them I had cleared the written examination and wanted to attend my SSB interview at Allahabad. Coincidentally, my call letter was from 11 SSB Allahabad, a board widely known among aspirants as one of the toughest SSB boards in the country.

After a lot of convincing, my parents reluctantly agreed. Deep down, they believed I probably wouldn't make it through. Honestly, I wasn't sure either.

Around 120 candidates were expected to report, but only about 75 actually arrived. I was allotted Chest Number 37, almost right in the middle. We went through the Officer Intelligence Rating (OIR) tests and the Picture Perception and Discussion Test (PPDT). When the screening results were announced, I had successfully cleared the first stage.

At that moment, I knew I would be staying for the complete five-day selection process.

Interestingly, I wasn't carrying the pressure of desperately wanting to get selected. My mindset was simple: *If I get recommended, that's wonderful. If not, I'll still enjoy every single day of this incredible experience.*

After screening, I was given a new identity—Chest Number 17—and assigned to one of the seven groups.

The psychological tests came next. Even today, I'm not sure whether my stories were exceptional or just average. I completed all the story-writing tasks, attempted around 45–50 Situation Reaction Test (SRT) questions, and performed reasonably well in the remaining psychology assessments.

The third day brought the Group Testing Officer (GTO) tasks, which, in my opinion, went quite well. During the lecturette, I spoke confidently. I managed to complete around twelve individual obstacles and performed well in the Snake Race, where I was able to demonstrate my physical fitness and contribute effectively to my team's performance.

Before the conference, my group happened to be the last one scheduled for the personal interview.

The interview itself was a memorable experience. The Interviewing Officer appreciated many of my responses but also scolded me a little because I kept switching from English to Hindi. Looking back, that advice was invaluable. If you come from a background where you've had access to good education and opportunities, the officers expect you to communicate confidently in English. My advice to every serious NDA aspirant is simple: work on your spoken English. It truly matters during the interview.

The officer also asked a few tricky questions that challenged my thinking, but overall, it turned out to be one of the best conversations I had ever experienced.

Then came the conference—the final stage.

Ironically, this was the moment that almost made me cry, even before the results were announced.

During the conference, one of the officers asked me what I had learned over the previous few days. I gave what I now consider some of the worst answers imaginable. Even today, I feel embarrassed whenever I think about them. Those responses still make me cringe, and I would probably never repeat them publicly.

After stepping outside the conference room, I was convinced my journey had ended there.

Finally, all the candidates assembled for the results.

Everyone stood silently, waiting for the Technical Officer to announce the recommended chest numbers.

I had already accepted that my name wouldn't be called.

The officer began reading:

**"Chest Number 7..."**

**"Chest Number 8..."**

Then came:

**"Chest Number 17."**

For a moment, I looked around, wondering if I had heard it correctly.

Was that really me?

The realization hit me.

I couldn't stop smiling.

I couldn't stop laughing.

All the nervousness, all the uncertainty, every obstacle I had faced suddenly disappeared in that one moment.

The officer continued announcing a few more chest numbers, and finally, only **five candidates** had been recommended from our batch.

I was one of them.

From nearly **6 lakh students** who had appeared for the NDA written examination, only around **8,132** received SSB calls. Out of those thousands of candidates, only **735** were finally recommended.

And somehow...

I was among those **735**.

My All India Rank was **496**, earning me a place at the National Defence Academy.

Looking back today, I don't believe my journey was extraordinary because I was exceptionally talented.

It was extraordinary because I refused to let fear make the decision for me.

I almost never got the chance to even fill out the NDA form. Had I listened to my fears—or accepted that my circumstances defined my future—I would never have known what I was capable of.

This journey taught me that miracles don't happen because luck chooses a few people. They happen because sometimes, despite uncertainty, despite opposition, and despite the odds, you choose to take that one step forward.

And sometimes...

that's all destiny needs.
